title: Persona Decision Report: Self-Serve Failed-Payment Exception Reports

input.idea: A narrow self-serve tool for three small SaaS founders who each pay EUR 250 per month for a manually produced failed-payment exception report, send the same Stripe CSV every Monday, and have asked for a faster self-serve version.
input.target_user: One of three small SaaS founders already paying for the weekly manual report
input.use_case: Upload the standard Stripe export during Monday finance review and receive the same failed-payment exception report in five minutes
input.current_alternative: Send a CSV to the founder-operator and wait for the manually reconciled exception report
input.price: EUR 250 per month for the existing manual service
input.user_evidence: Three founders pay EUR 250 monthly, send the same Stripe CSV every Monday, and have asked for self-serve access because the manual report arrives too slowly.

normalized_idea: Convert an existing paid weekly service into a narrow self-serve importer that accepts the same Stripe export and reproduces the same exception report faster for the current three customers.
tldr: Persona evaluated a self-serve version of an existing paid failed-payment reporting service and recommends Build with medium confidence. Three customers already pay for the manual workflow and have asked for faster access, so the narrow automation has a credible first market. Build the smallest importer for one current customer and verify that its output matches the manual report before expanding.

evidence.observation: Three small SaaS founders each currently pay EUR 250 per month for the manually produced report.
evidence.implication: The first users, budget, and existing paid behavior are already observable.

evidence.observation: Each customer sends the same Stripe CSV every Monday and waits for reconciliation.
evidence.implication: The job repeats on a stable cadence with a known input and output.

evidence.observation: All three paying founders asked for self-serve access because the manual report arrives too slowly.
evidence.implication: The requested wedge addresses a stated latency problem rather than an invented adjacent feature.

evidence.observation: The proposed first version imports the same export and returns the same exception report rather than replacing subscription finance operations.
evidence.implication: A narrow first build can be compared directly against the paid manual baseline.

adversarial_dimensions.name: monthly failed-payment volume
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: Volume determines whether five-minute self-service materially changes the weekly finance workload.

adversarial_dimensions.name: exception complexity
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: Simple status grouping and account-specific judgment require different automation boundaries.

adversarial_dimensions.name: finance review cadence
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: The value of faster output rises when the report drives a fixed operating review.

adversarial_dimensions.name: Stripe export consistency
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: Input drift determines whether a narrow importer remains reliable without service work.

adversarial_dimensions.name: recovery urgency
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: Time-sensitive recovery actions determine the cost of waiting for a manual report.

adversarial_dimensions.name: pilot authority
adversarial_dimensions.rationale: The existing buyer's ability to accept the output and continue paying controls rollout speed.

adversarial_positions.label: Low-volume ad hoc reviewer
adversarial_positions.objection: Low failure volume and ad hoc review may not justify a product even though the current service is paid.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The customer still chooses self-serve in four consecutive weeks and continues paying the existing price.

adversarial_positions.label: Weekly rules-based operator
adversarial_positions.objection: The workflow fits, but rule exceptions must match the manual report without founder intervention.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: Four weekly diffs reach at least 95% classification agreement.

adversarial_positions.label: Context-heavy monthly account
adversarial_positions.objection: Account context absent from the export could make a monthly user wait for manual review anyway.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: Every required context field is either in the export or surfaced as an explicit review item.

adversarial_positions.label: Multi-account founder with input drift
adversarial_positions.objection: Multiple Stripe accounts can turn the importer into weekly normalization work.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The first supported account shape completes four runs with no manual column repair.

adversarial_positions.label: High-volume same-day recovery
adversarial_positions.objection: High urgency makes speed valuable, but one wrong grouping can trigger incorrect recovery action at scale.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The founder signs off on every action category after a parallel run.

adversarial_positions.label: Judgment-heavy analyst
adversarial_positions.objection: If most exceptions need manual judgment, self-service only moves the waiting point into an unresolved queue.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: Fewer than 10% of exceptions require context outside the export.

adversarial_positions.label: Stable export with limited authority
adversarial_positions.objection: A clean import is insufficient if the operator cannot approve use of the generated recovery list.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The budget owner reviews the parallel output and authorizes the weekly workflow.

adversarial_positions.label: Daily finance operator
adversarial_positions.objection: Daily cadence strengthens value, but it could pull the product toward live integrations before the CSV wedge is proven.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The customer accepts scheduled CSV use for the pilot without requesting OAuth.

adversarial_positions.label: Cash-risk account needing context
adversarial_positions.objection: Cash urgency raises value and the cost of a wrong exception classification simultaneously.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: Parallel reports agree on high-value accounts and the founder approves escalation rules.

adversarial_positions.label: Custom-enrichment workflow
adversarial_positions.objection: A customer-specific enrichment step would recreate the manual service and break the five-minute promise.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The supported export alone produces the accepted report or explicitly rejects the case.

adversarial_positions.label: Founder-controlled weekly rollout
adversarial_positions.objection: The buyer, cadence, and paid baseline align, but continued payment after automation remains unproven.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The founder runs four cycles unassisted and renews at EUR 250.

adversarial_positions.label: Suite-expansion request
adversarial_positions.objection: Requests beyond the exception report can distract from the paid workflow the evidence actually supports.
adversarial_positions.proof_trigger: The customer adopts the narrow report before any adjacent feature is scheduled.

decision_factors.name: Reachable first users
decision_factors.reason: The first users are the three current paying customers, not a hypothetical segment.

decision_factors.name: Repeated costly behavior
decision_factors.reason: The same export and reconciliation job recur every Monday and currently require paid manual work.

decision_factors.name: Credible switching reason
decision_factors.reason: Customers explicitly asked to remove the manual waiting time while preserving the known report.

decision_factors.name: Narrow verifiable wedge
decision_factors.reason: The first build has a stable input and can be diffed against an accepted manual output.

decision_factors.name: Accuracy without hidden service work
decision_factors.reason: The supplied evidence does not yet show how much account context and judgment the manual reconciliation embeds.

hard_nos.title: Hidden manual judgment could make the importer dishonest
hard_nos.objection: If the accepted report depends on account history or founder judgment absent from the CSV, a fast upload flow will silently produce a worse product.
hard_nos.why_it_matters: The wedge is valuable only if it preserves the trusted exceptions, not merely the report format.
hard_nos.response: Diff every generated line against four weeks of manual reports and expose any rule that still requires review.

hard_nos.title: Input drift can recreate the service business
hard_nos.objection: Multiple accounts, renamed columns, or custom enrichment can turn each upload into founder-operated cleanup.
hard_nos.why_it_matters: A self-serve product that needs weekly repair has not removed delivery latency or labor.
hard_nos.response: Support only the exact export used by the first pilot and reject unsupported shapes explicitly.

hard_nos.title: Adjacent finance features can erase the proven wedge
hard_nos.objection: Expanding into dunning, messaging, forecasting, or multi-processor reconciliation before matching the paid report replaces direct demand with roadmap speculation.
hard_nos.why_it_matters: The evidence supports one report and one latency improvement, not a revenue-operations suite.
hard_nos.response: Freeze scope until one customer runs the importer unassisted for four weekly cycles and keeps paying.

recommendation.decisive_reason: Three reachable customers already pay for the repeated workflow and have requested the exact latency-reducing self-serve wedge proposed for the first build.
evidence_limits: The behavior and requests are founder-supplied and have not been independently reviewed.
No automated output has yet been compared line by line with the accepted manual report.
Retention after replacing the founder's service labor is unproven.
reversal_evidence: Historical report diffs show that the CSV lacks critical context for most exceptions.
The first paying founder refuses to use the importer without broad adjacent finance features.
A four-week pilot requires recurring manual cleanup or produces materially different recovery actions.

mistakes_to_avoid: Do not add live Stripe OAuth before the existing CSV import proves the output.
Do not hide unsupported exceptions; route them visibly to manual review.
Do not replace the paid service for all three customers at once.
Do not measure success by uploads when the accepted baseline is report accuracy, turnaround, and continued payment.
Do not broaden beyond failed-payment exceptions during the four-week pilot.

next_action.action: Build the CSV-to-exception-report path for one paying founder and run it beside the manual report for the next four Mondays.
next_action.why_now: The comparison tests the only material unknown: whether the known input contains enough information to reproduce the trusted output without hidden service work.
next_action.success_threshold: For four consecutive weeks, the founder completes the upload unassisted, receives the report within five minutes, at least 95% of exception classifications match the accepted manual report, and the EUR 250 monthly payment continues.
next_action.kill_threshold: Stop productizing this wedge if two weekly runs need more than 30 minutes of manual cleanup or if more than 10% of exceptions require context unavailable from the supported export.
